materials.name: Ambroxan
materials.wholesale_check: Real disagreement across sources, not a clean verification: our $704-1,238/kg range sits ABOVE industrial bulk B2B trade data (~$61/kg for premium-grade powder per reedintelligence/accio.com 2026 trade analysis), but within range of small-quantity boutique 'true KAO Ambroxan' crystal retail (perfumersupplyhouse.com shows wide $8-63 for small packs, scaling up plausibly to four figures per kg). The $350-590/kg figure originally cited (premierepeau) appears to be a third, middle tier. This material has genuinely fragmented pricing by purity/brand/volume tier - treat any single number with caution.
materials.wholesale_source: Cross-source reference table (perfumesociety, premierepeau, ads-perfumes, alphaaromatics) - June 2026

materials.name: BASIL OIL (METHYLCHAVICOL TYPE) METHYL EUGENOL FREE
Black Pepper Oil
materials.notes: No single acute 2026 event, but a real, well-documented STRUCTURAL risk: Indonesian patchouli sustainability is under genuine long-term threat from over-farming and lack of agronomic best practices (no fertilizer/bedding standards, minimal R&D base unlike coffee/cacao/palm oil) - industry sources describe this as an ongoing crisis in slow motion, not a single shock. Separately, a Jan 2026 trade-policy study found Indonesian cinnamon/cassia exports underperform due to non-tariff barriers (food safety/technical standards), a real structural market-access constraint. Clove and vetiver supply appear more stable by comparison.

materials.name: CARDAMOM ABSOLUTE
materials.notes: Real, ongoing tightness confirmed via April-May 2026 trade sources (Mundus Agri): coriander seeds in tight supply with rising prices; high-quality cardamom in short supply; weather conditions in Andhra Pradesh/Telangana affecting related spice crops. This is more than routine logistics friction - it reflects real acreage decline and weather-driven tightness across multiple Indian spice crops simultaneously. Separately, Indian crude oil import capacity has shown real vulnerability in 2026 (single-supplier volume drops of 70%+ during regional disruptions), a relevant background risk for any petrochemical-route synthetic, though not specific to these spice-derived naturals.

CLOVE TERPENES
materials.notes: Madagascar (Sava region, ~80% of world vanilla supply) hit by Cyclone Gezani, Feb 2026 - landfall as Cat 3 equivalent, ~$2B damage, 59 dead in-country. Came 10 days after Cyclone Fytia. Vanilla orchids take ~4 years to reach harvest, so repeated storm damage compounds across seasons. Clove (same Sava region) likely faces logistics/labor disruption even though the plant itself is less storm-fragile than vanilla vines.

materials.name: GINGER P GRND
materials.notes: Real, severe, multi-country crisis as of mid-2026, NOT reflected by the India region tag alone. China's crop is 40-50% lower than last year with prices roughly doubled. Peru's exports down ~43% since 2024 due to high shipping/farming costs. Costa Rica lost ~40% of crop to bacterial disease. Ghana's 2026 harvest is already lost to a persistent crop disease, with the pathogen expected to linger in soil for years. India is a potential beneficiary picking up export slack, not a crisis source for this material - the previous note (India container/freight costs) did not apply to Ginger specifically and has been corrected here.

materials.name: TINOGARD AS (light stabilizer OS) or LOWILITE 26
Vanilla Bourbon SFE Organic (concentrated extract, NOT raw beans)
materials.notes: Nuanced situation, not a simple one-directional crisis. Real crop damage: Cyclone Gezani hit Madagascar's Sava region (~80% of world vanilla supply) Feb 2026. However, broader market data shows the OPPOSITE of acute crisis pricing right now - wholesale running just $6-24/kg in 2026 (vs. historical extreme of ~$600/kg after 2017's cyclone crisis), and Madagascar recently LIFTED its $250/kg minimum export price floor due to oversupply from strong prior harvests. Real risk exists (climate vulnerability, ~4yr crop cycle meaning damage compounds slowly) but current pricing reflects a low/oversupply phase, not acute scarcity. NOTE: these two priced entries are concentrated extracts (SFE, 3X), not raw beans - their high $/kg reflects concentration factor, not crisis pricing.
materials.wholesale_check: Raw Madagascar vanilla beans: $6-24/kg wholesale (2026); concentrated SFE extracts are legitimately much higher due to concentration factor - our price is plausible for an SFE specifically, not for raw beans
